title: Incidents runbook: overload behavior with tiered batching defaults (fast/standard/cost-efficient)
preview:
## Goal
Provide a single on-call runbook for diagnosing and mitigating overload-related latency regressions when tiered batching defaults are enabled (fast / standard / cost-efficient). This runbook covers:
- symptom → likely cause mapping
- immediate mitigations (no code)
- tier-specific guidance
- rollback / kill-...
text:
## Goal
Provide a single on-call runbook for diagnosing and mitigating overload-related latency regressions when tiered batching defaults are enabled (fast / standard / cost-efficient). This runbook covers:
- symptom → likely cause mapping
- immediate mitigations (no code)
- tier-specific guidance
- rollback / kill-switch procedures
- post-incident follow-ups

This is intended for Runtime + SRE on-call during staged rollout (internal canary → hosted subset → Dedicated opt-in → broader default).

---

## Definitions / mental model
**Overload** here means one (or more) of:
- arrival rate > sustainable throughput for the active tier profile
- queue growth that increases time-to-first-token (TTFT) and/or p99 end-to-end latency
- admission control/load shedding triggers (or fails to trigger)
- scheduler behavior that starves some requests under mixed prompt length / mixed streaming

**Tier...

title: Define graduated pin-matrix and compatibility notes for new open model intake
preview: Problem: Recent intake of community weights shows wide variance in runtime behavior across variants (KV cache sizing, attention kernel compatibility, and sequence-length tail cases). Current onboarding guidance is ambiguous about when a model should be fully pinned versus soft-pinned or allowed to float with compati...
text:
Problem: Recent intake of community weights shows wide variance in runtime behavior across variants (KV cache sizing, attention kernel compatibility, and sequence-length tail cases). Current onboarding guidance is ambiguous about when a model should be fully pinned versus soft-pinned or allowed to float with compatibility annotations. This ambiguity causes runtime teams to either over-pin (increasing operational burden) or under-pin (causing production regressions).

Goal: Clarify admission requirements and produce a graduated 'pin matrix' and a compatibility-notes template that PMs/engineers/DS can populate during intake. The matrix should map risk signals (kernel incompatibility, quantization sensitivity, memory volatility, eval regression risk) to recommended pin states: Unpinned, Soft-Pin (compat advisory + auto-fallback), and Hard-Pin (runtime-locked).

Scope:
- Define the pin ma...

title: Release Contrast Workbench and Auto-Fallback Playbook
preview: Build a Release Contrast Workbench inside Console that lets engineers inspect behavioral and cost differences between two or more model variants (control, variant, mirrored) for a given route and time window, and orchestrate an automated fallback when a regression is detected. The workbench combines: per-route time-...
text: Build a Release Contrast Workbench inside Console that lets engineers inspect behavioral and cost differences between two or more model variants (control, variant, mirrored) for a given route and time window, and orchestrate an automated fallback when a regression is detected. The workbench combines: per-route time-series and histogram deltas, request-level trace-linking to compare paired requests, token-level cost and latency deltas, structured-output diffs (JSON/func-call fields), and a policy editor for automatic fallback/rollback thresholds. This ticket defines scope, success criteria, and a delivery plan. Key constraints: must integrate with existing telemetry pipeline (span and metric schemas), support both hosted and Dedicated deployments, and be low-friction in the UI for fast incident response.

title: PagerDuty signal aggregator and compact runbook injector for multi-scope incidents
preview: Problem: When multi-scope incidents (e.g., cross-service latency spikes affecting both hosted and dedicated customers) are triggered, current PagerDuty alerts are noisy and lack synthesized context. Engineers either receive many small PD alerts or a single opaque signal without the right evidence. Runbooks are stati...
text:
Problem: When multi-scope incidents (e.g., cross-service latency spikes affecting both hosted and dedicated customers) are triggered, current PagerDuty alerts are noisy and lack synthesized context. Engineers either receive many small PD alerts or a single opaque signal without the right evidence. Runbooks are static links; injecting the compact, incident-specific runbook snippets and relevant trace/metric slices into the initial PD attachment would reduce mean-time-to-ack and mean-time-to-resolve.

Goal: Build a PD signal aggregator service that consumes observability events (traces, metric slices, error logs), performs lightweight evidence bundling and anomaly scoring, and emits a single enriched PagerDuty incident attachment. The attachment will include: a one-paragraph synthesis, top-3 likely root causes (service/region/model-variant), prioritized trace snippets (up to 3), the bes...

title: OpsWorklet: Synthetic runbook task adapter and ephemeral admin sandboxes
preview: Problem: On-call and support engineers need a reproducible, safe environment to run production runbook steps without risking real tenant systems. Current runbooks are prose or ad-hoc scripts; replays are flaky and hard to test. Goal: Build OpsWorklet, a lightweight adapter that converts runbook steps into determinis...
text: Problem: On-call and support engineers need a reproducible, safe environment to run production runbook steps without risking real tenant systems. Current runbooks are prose or ad-hoc scripts; replays are flaky and hard to test. Goal: Build OpsWorklet, a lightweight adapter that converts runbook steps into deterministic, instrumented tasks that run inside ephemeral admin sandboxes. The adapter will provide: 1) deterministic replay using recorded event traces and fixture data, 2) sandbox isolation via Kubernetes namespaces and short-lived RBAC tokens, 3) safety rails that enforce read-only defaults and require explicit allowlists for any mutating step, 4) integrated auditing that writes actions and parameters to our audit log, 5) an operator-facing CLI and Console panel to spawn, inspect, and promote sandboxes to canary runs. Implementation outline: - A runbook DSL parser that converts ...

title: Emergent model-flavor dossier and competitive sentiment log

text:
Objective: Produce a condensed dossier that captures newly emergent 'model flavors' across major open and hosted LLM providers, synthesize commercial and technical sentiment, and deliver prioritized tactical recommendations for Redwood inference (serving/runtime, fallback policies, and Dedicated capacity playbook).

Scope and deliverables:

- Catalog of 12+ emergent model flavors (e.g., short-context optimized variants, prompt-steering-resistant checkpoints, compact quantized discontinuities) with sample prompts and failure modes.
- Gap map: which flavors our runtime supports today (batching/KV cache/quantization) and which need engineering investments.
- Competitive sentiment log summarizing partner calls, GitHub/issue tracker signal, vendor pricing moves, and observed lat...

title: decision-proxy-for-rbac-v2-with-audit-streaming-and-operator-ui-controls
preview:
Summary:
Build a lightweight authorization decision proxy (ADP) that sits in front of API/runtime/console entry points to centralize RBAC v2 evaluation, stream structured audit events in near real-time, and expose a small operator UI for emergency allow/deny overrides. The ADP is intended to be a minimal, high-perfo...
text:
Summary:
Build a lightweight authorization decision proxy (ADP) that sits in front of API/runtime/console entry points to centralize RBAC v2 evaluation, stream structured audit events in near real-time, and expose a small operator UI for emergency allow/deny overrides. The ADP is intended to be a minimal, high-performance layer that defers policy compilation and heavy logic to the existing policy service but provides caching, request-level context enrichment (SAML/SSO attributes, request tags), and an auditable override workflow for on-call.

Goals:
- Centralize decision routing to reduce duplicated policy checks across runtime and console.
- Emit structured audit events (protobuf/JSON) for every authorization decision with correlation IDs for tracing.
- Provide a small operator UI enabling time-bound emergency allow/deny for in-flight requests or session tokens, with full audit trail...

text:
## Context
We are shipping tiered batching defaults (fast / standard / cost-efficient). We need safe rollout primitives to enable this progressively and to immediately disable on regression without requiring a full runtime rollback.

This ticket implements:
1) Feature flagging to gate the new default-selection path.
2) Staged rollout controls (percent-based + scope-based) for Hosted and Dedicated.
3) Immediate kill switch that forces the system back to legacy/manual batching behavior (or “standard” fallback) in < 1 minute.

This must be compatible with:
- Reason codes / telemetry (ENG-18426)
- Tier config schema + precedence (ENG-18422, ENG-18430)
- Perf canary + SLO gates (ENG-18428)
- Dashboards/alerts to drive rollback decisions (ENG-18429)

## Goals
- Reduce blast radius: allow ramp from internal canary → small hosted slice → larger hosted → Dedicated opt-in → broader default.
- M...

text:
Problem
We need a canonical, computed “error budget state” per SLO (tier x region x endpoint class) to support:
- Console budget status + drilldowns
- Burn summaries (incident-bot)
- Deploy gates (warn-only -> enforcement)
- Post-incident accounting (how much budget was spent, when, and by what SLI)

This ticket builds/ships the computation pipeline that produces budget_remaining + budget_status on a rolling window with data quality guards and a backfill strategy.

Scope
In-scope for Q1 2026 rollout:
- Hosted (shared) + Dedicated (reserved capacity) tiers
- Endpoints: chat/completions, embeddings, streaming
- Regions: US + EU core regions (as defined in SLO config)
- SLIs: success rate (non-5xx per spec), latency SLI (requests under threshold), streaming reliability (disconnect/error events)

Out of scope (explicitly):
- Private tier enforcement (may reuse same machinery later)

title: Perf canary thresholds for FP8/INT8 rollout (noise handling + auto rollback)
preview:
Implement perf-canary gating for FP8/INT8 kernel enablement so we can safely roll out kernel-selection changes by model/region/GPU cohort. Scope includes: threshold profiles (TTFT/TPOT/tokens-s/error rate), noise suppression (small sample + baseline drift), and automatic rollback triggers with clear runbook steps.

...
text:
Implement perf-canary gating for FP8/INT8 kernel enablement so we can safely roll out kernel-selection changes by model/region/GPU cohort. Scope includes: threshold profiles (TTFT/TPOT/tokens-s/error rate), noise suppression (small sample + baseline drift), and automatic rollback triggers with clear runbook steps.

Why: We saw early TTFT regressions in limited prompt regimes during kernel bring-up and want a reliable automated guardrail before expanding beyond canary regions. Additionally, current canary alerts are too noisy in low-traffic cohorts; need min-sample + aggregation rules to avoid false positives while still catching real regressions.

Key links:
- Confluence runbook (target doc to update/validate): sources/confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runbooks/perf-canary-rollout-for-new-kernels.json
- Metric definitions reference: sources/google_drive/shared_drives/engineering/observab...

title: Suppression windows + admin override flow for SLO-triggered auto actions (with audit logging)
preview:
Context
Auto-rollback/pause is being wired to SLO burn-rate evaluation. We need a safe, auditable way to suppress auto actions during known incidents / telemetry issues / planned experiments, and an admin-only override path that is time-bound and visible in Console + logs.

Problem
Without suppression/override contr...
text:
Context
Auto-rollback/pause is being wired to SLO burn-rate evaluation. We need a safe, auditable way to suppress auto actions during known incidents / telemetry issues / planned experiments, and an admin-only override path that is time-bound and visible in Console + logs.

Problem
Without suppression/override controls:
- Oncall cannot quickly stop repeated auto actions during a known incident (or during monitor/telemetry degradation), causing churn and noise.
- We risk either disabling the feature broadly (losing protection) or allowing unsafe rollbacks (SLA risk).
- Compliance and incident retros need an explicit audit trail for: who suppressed, why, for what scope, and when it expired.

Goals
1) Suppression windows
- Allow creating a suppression that prevents auto actions (rollback/pause/traffic-shift) for a specific scope.
- Scope must support at minimum: service/route + model + r...

text:
Context

title: Perf-canary post-launch noise reduction (smoothing + baseline drift + region/SKU thresholds)
preview:
Context / problem
- After phase-1 rollout (us-east + limited model set) and enabling warning + paging alerts, we saw a spike in false positives. Primary drivers were:
 1) Baseline drift (slow change in fleet + scheduler behavior) causing “regression” deltas that were within normal variation.
 2) Region + hardware SK...
text:
Context / problem
- After phase-1 rollout (us-east + limited model set) and enabling warning + paging alerts, we saw a spike in false positives. Primary drivers were:
 1) Baseline drift (slow change in fleet + scheduler behavior) causing “regression” deltas that were within normal variation.
 2) Region + hardware SKU variance: same model behaves differently across SKUs (and even within region depending on pool composition), so global thresholds were too tight for some deployments and too loose for others.
 3) Cardinality/schema churn: small label changes caused baseline lookups to miss (or compare apples-to-oranges) and resulted in spurious percent-change comparisons.
 4) Telemetry ingestion lag: late-arriving histograms produced misleading rolling-window stats.

Goal
Reduce false positives without materially reducing sensitivity to real regressions.

Non-goals (for this ticket)
- Bui...

title: Private deployments: sync regional failover guidance with new trigger hierarchy + automation guardrails
preview:

We updated Hosted API + Dedicated regional failover automation (signal hierarchy, deterministic policy evaluation, rollback guardrails, dry-run/simulate tooling). Our Private deployment guidance still reflects the pre-upgrade world (manual runbooks + ad-hoc triggers), which is now out of sync and confusin...
text:
## Context
We updated Hosted API + Dedicated regional failover automation (signal hierarchy, deterministic policy evaluation, rollback guardrails, dry-run/simulate tooling). Our Private deployment guidance still reflects the pre-upgrade world (manual runbooks + ad-hoc triggers), which is now out of sync and confusing for regulated / VPC / on-prem customers.

This ticket aligns Private docs with the new approach:
- Make it explicit what Redwood supports vs. what we recommend customers implement.
- Translate trigger hierarchy + anti-flap/rollback semantics into Private-friendly patterns (where customers may own routing + health evaluation).
- Ensure we are consistent with security boundaries (data residency, audit logs) and avoid implying unsupported automation.

## Goals
1) Reduce support load from ambiguous expectations (“Does Redwood automatically fail over my Private region?”).
2) P...

text:
We need a single, auditable drift SLI definition for the Standard Cost Model initiative, plus a monitoring pipeline that continuously compares predicted (cost-model outputs) vs observed (infra spend / GPU-seconds-derived cost) and alerts when drift exceeds thresholds. This is required before Console flips cost breakdowns broadly and before Finance treats standard cost outputs as a reliable unit-econ primitive.

Context: We already saw a drift alert fire due to telemetry lag (see incidents thread 1769032199). This ticket formalizes drift semantics, adds lag-awareness + volume guardrails, and ships actionable dashboards + paging rules.

Deliverables:
- Drift SLI definition (v1) with explicit formulae, inclusion/exclusion rules, and windowing/lag policy.
- Scheduled drift monitoring job/pipeline emitting Prometheus metrics (bounded cardinality).

title: Update API gateway timeouts/retries and circuit breakers so failover doesn’t amplify thundering herd
preview:
Context
During regional impairment + automated routing failover, our current gateway behavior can multiply load:
- clients retry (often aggressively) on timeouts/5xx
- gateway retries upstream on a subset of failures
- routing may redirect to another region while the original region is partially impaired

Net: thund...
text:
Context
During regional impairment + automated routing failover, our current gateway behavior can multiply load:
- clients retry (often aggressively) on timeouts/5xx
- gateway retries upstream on a subset of failures
- routing may redirect to another region while the original region is partially impaired

Net: thundering herd risk and cascading overload on the target region, plus extended tail latency while the system is trying to be “helpful”. This is a recurring pattern in incidents and showed up during rehearsals as elevated retries + timeouts right when failover begins.

Goal
Tune gateway retry/timeouts + circuit breaker behavior so the gateway fails fast and predictably during impairment, and so we shed load in a way that cooperates with routing/failover automation.

Non-goals
- Rewriting client SDK retry behavior (tracked separately in docs/SDK follow-ups)
- Changing customer SL...

Context

- This ticket defines *measurement boundaries* + *what counts* (and what is excluded) for success rate, latency, and streaming reliability across core API request paths.

In scope endpoints (initial)
1) Chat/Completions (non-streaming)
2) Chat/Completions (streaming via SSE)
3) Embeddings (non-streaming)

Tiering
- SLIs MUST be computed per tier label (Hosted vs Dedicated) and region.
- Tier label is treated as authoritative based on request context set at ingress and propagated through routing/fallback (see dependency ENG-4922).

title: Regional failover automation upgrade (Hosted API + Dedicated) — umbrella
preview:
## Context
Today, regional failover is a mix of manual actions and partially automated routing policies. Detection can be noisy (false positives), real regional impairment can be slow to identify, and end-to-end safety validation is hard.

This umbrella tracks the Reliability Program work to:
- Improve trigger signa...
text:
## Context
Today, regional failover is a mix of manual actions and partially automated routing policies. Detection can be noisy (false positives), real regional impairment can be slow to identify, and end-to-end safety validation is hard.

This umbrella tracks the Reliability Program work to:
- Improve trigger signal pipeline (reachability, error burn, tail latency burn, capacity headroom, network symptoms)
- Add guardrails + deterministic policy evaluation (avoid flapping/oscillation)
- Validate via controlled failover tests (“game days”) with measurable SLO outcomes
- Improve observability so on-call can quickly distinguish region impairment vs local service issues

## Goals (success criteria)
- Detection + failover time reduced vs baseline (target: automated recommendation within minutes; execution gated by safety limits/optional approval hooks)
- Fewer false-positive regional trig...

title: Perf canary: alerting policy + on-call routing (warn/page) and incident-bot integration
preview:
Goal
Define and implement production alert rules + routing for the perf regression canary (per-model), including incident-bot formatting/dedupe, and explicit anti-flap/noise-budget requirements.

Context
We are deploying an always-on synthetic canary in production to detect p95 latency regressions and tokens/sec dro...
text:
Goal
Define and implement production alert rules + routing for the perf regression canary (per-model), including incident-bot formatting/dedupe, and explicit anti-flap/noise-budget requirements.

Context
We are deploying an always-on synthetic canary in production to detect p95 latency regressions and tokens/sec drops per model, with attribution signals (queueing vs runtime execution, kernel selection tags, runtime build tags).

This ticket covers:
- Warning vs paging thresholds and when they apply (top models vs all models)
- Required anti-flap: min sample size, sustained windows, dampening, cooldowns
- Notification routing: #eng-runtime vs #eng-oncall, plus escalation matrix
- Incident-bot integration: payload fields, dedupe keys, thread formatting, and links to dashboards/runbook
- Noise budget: maximum pages/warnings per day/week, plus what to tune when exceeded

Non-goals (for th...

title: Runbook refresh: regional failover + Dedicated drain procedures aligned to new automation/rollback
preview:
Why
- Rehearsals + Game Day 1 surfaced that existing runbooks still describe manual failover steps and don’t match the new controller behavior (dry-run, approvals, anti-flap timers, rollback semantics, per-tenant safety limits).
- Dedicated drain guidance also needs to reflect new sequencing/guardrails so we don’t o...
text:
Why
- Rehearsals + Game Day 1 surfaced that existing runbooks still describe manual failover steps and don’t match the new controller behavior (dry-run, approvals, anti-flap timers, rollback semantics, per-tenant safety limits).
- Dedicated drain guidance also needs to reflect new sequencing/guardrails so we don’t overload the target region or strand dedicated workloads.

Scope (this ticket)
Update the on-call runbooks + operator guide entry points so they reflect the new end-to-end workflow:
1) Detect/confirm regional impairment using standardized signals (reachability > 5xx burn > tail latency burn > capacity).
2) Use failover controller in dry-run to preview recommended actions + evidence.
3) Execute with approval hooks (prod), respecting rate limits/anti-flap and per-tenant safety constraints.
4) Validate traffic shift + customer impact + SLO burn.
5) Drain/evacuate GPU pools (Hos...

title: Dedicated pool drain automation for regional failover workflows (with safety gates)
preview:
Context
Dedicated customers run on reserved GPU pools with isolation requirements; when a region is impaired or we execute a controlled failover, we need a safe, repeatable way to drain / evacuate Dedicated pools without causing cascading overload, thrashing routing policies, or violating per-tenant availability ass...
text:
Context
Dedicated customers run on reserved GPU pools with isolation requirements; when a region is impaired or we execute a controlled failover, we need a safe, repeatable way to drain / evacuate Dedicated pools without causing cascading overload, thrashing routing policies, or violating per-tenant availability assumptions.

Today (baseline)
- Drain actions are manual / semi-manual (a mix of ad-hoc scripts + operator judgement).
- Sequencing is not standardized: routing flips sometimes happen before pools are actually able to shed load, leading to transient 5xx/timeouts and noisy alerts.
- Limited guardrails: no hard limits on how much traffic is being displaced concurrently, and limited visibility into drain progress.

Goal
Automate Dedicated pool drain and evacuation as a first-class step in the regional failover workflow, with explicit safety gates and observability. This should w...

title: Fix streaming reconnect during region failover/redirect (idempotency + resume)
preview:
Problem
During regional impairment and automated routing flips, a non-trivial portion of streaming requests (SSE) are terminated mid-stream. Many clients treat this as a hard failure and re-issue the request, which can:
- Duplicate generation (double-charging tokens + duplicated assistant output)
- Increase thunderi...
text:
Problem
During regional impairment and automated routing flips, a non-trivial portion of streaming requests (SSE) are terminated mid-stream. Many clients treat this as a hard failure and re-issue the request, which can:
- Duplicate generation (double-charging tokens + duplicated assistant output)
- Increase thundering-herd behavior during failover windows
- Cause confusing UX for end-users (partial stream then restart)

We saw this clearly during staging rehearsals and in the 2026-01-15 controlled failover. Support also received reports of streaming disconnects during region redirect.

Scope
This issue is focused on Hosted API streaming behavior when a request is redirected cross-region (policy-driven failover) OR when the gateway returns overload/redirect responses during failover.

Observed symptoms
- SSE stream disconnects on redirect boundary; some clients do not follow redirects ...

title: Control-plane managed owner-mapping config (versioned + audited) for incident taxonomy
preview: Create a control-plane-managed ownership mapping config for incident taxonomy (type/subtype -> primary owner, secondary/consulted, escalation channel, runbook links). Config must be versioned, reviewed, auditable, and safely rolled out/rolled back. This mapping is used by incident-bot and alert/paging routing to red...
text: Create a control-plane-managed ownership mapping config for incident taxonomy (type/subtype -> primary owner, secondary/consulted, escalation channel, runbook links). Config must be versioned, reviewed, auditable, and safely rolled out/rolled back. This mapping is used by incident-bot and alert/paging routing to reduce ambiguity and improve MTTR.

Background

text:
Tracking ticket for issues found during Game Day 1 (controlled production failover: us-east → eu-west) and the immediate hardening work required before expanding automation to additional routes/models/tiers.

Background
- Test window: 2026-01-15 (see: Controlled Failover Test Results 2026-01-15; #incidents thread "controlled-failover-us-east-to-eu-west-live")
- Scope exercised: Hosted API + limited Dedicated tenants behind feature flags; dry-run + execute paths; rollback/hold-down timers; capacity gating; DNS TTL changes already applied.
- Outcome summary:
 - Automation executed the planned routing shift within target, but we observed (a) transient 5xx/timeouts for a subset of traffic, (b) elevated tail latency in eu-west immediately after shift, and (c) confusion on dashboards due to missing/late annotations.
 - One rollback was triggered sooner than expected due to hold-down/anti-fl...

Deliverables: written test plan, schedule/roles, dashboard checklist, succe...
text: Define concrete load test scenarios + success criteria to validate hot-route capacity protection (tier-aware rate limits/quotas, priority routing, admission control, controlled shedding) and coordinate execution of an on-call style game day. Deliverables: written test plan, schedule/roles, dashboard checklist, success/failure criteria, and post-exercise follow-ups.

title: Instrument routing fallback_reason enums and map to incident taxonomy
preview:
Problem
Routing-related incidents have been repeatedly misclassified during live response ("routing bug" vs "fleet capacity" vs "regional health"), which slows escalation and weakens reporting. Today, router emits a mix of freeform logs and opaque counters (e.g., "fallback=true") without a single, low-cardinality ex...
text:
Problem
Routing-related incidents have been repeatedly misclassified during live response ("routing bug" vs "fleet capacity" vs "regional health"), which slows escalation and weakens reporting. Today, router emits a mix of freeform logs and opaque counters (e.g., "fallback=true") without a single, low-cardinality explanation.

This issue adds a structured `fallback_reason` signal to router decisions (and downstream logs/metrics/traces) with a strict enum allowlist. We also define a deterministic mapping from `fallback_reason` (+ a small number of qualifiers) to the v1 incident taxonomy buckets so incident-bot and on-call can quickly classify and route to the correct primary owner.

Goals
- Add `fallback_reason` as a low-cardinality enum emitted on every request that uses a fallback path (model variant, region change, or pool change).
- Ensure we can distinguish at least: capacity, hea...
tags: Reliability Program: Incident taxonomy + ownership mapping

title: Game Day 1: controlled failover us-east → eu-west (Hosted API + Dedicated)
preview:
## Summary
Execute the first controlled production failover game day (us-east → eu-west) to validate end-to-end automation: trigger signal pipeline, deterministic policy evaluation, guardrails/rollback, circuit breakers/load shedding, observability, and comms.

This is an operational ticket for run-of-show, checklis...
text:
## Summary
Execute the first controlled production failover game day (us-east → eu-west) to validate end-to-end automation: trigger signal pipeline, deterministic policy evaluation, guardrails/rollback, circuit breakers/load shedding, observability, and comms.

This is an operational ticket for run-of-show, checklists, comms plan, and sign-offs.

## Scope (this game day)
- **Hosted API**: a limited set of routes/models behind feature flags; observe client behavior, streaming reconnect, gateway retries.
- **Dedicated**: a small set of opted-in test tenants/pools; validate drain/evacuation workflow gates and progress metrics.
- **Failover controller**: start in dry-run, then execute approved policy flips.

Out of scope: Private deployments (only validate documentation messaging if asked).

## Links / Artifacts
- Confluence plan: `sources/confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/game-days...

title: Add failover metrics + multi-region SLO burn-rate alerts for regional impairment detection
preview:
Context / Problem
Regional failover automation work (trigger hierarchy, controller, rollback) needs first-class observability so on-call can:
1) detect true regional impairment quickly (without paging on local/service-specific noise)
2) understand *why* the system is failing over (reachability vs 5xx burn vs latency...
text:
Context / Problem
Regional failover automation work (trigger hierarchy, controller, rollback) needs first-class observability so on-call can:
1) detect true regional impairment quickly (without paging on local/service-specific noise)
2) understand *why* the system is failing over (reachability vs 5xx burn vs latency burn vs capacity)
3) verify the outcome (did we reduce impact and stay within error budgets?)

Today we have partial metrics (gateway 5xx, some latency dashboards) but no standardized event metrics for failover decisions, and burn-rate alerts are inconsistent across Hosted API vs Dedicated routes/tiers. We also had staging rehearsal feedback that alerts were noisy and dashboards lacked automation annotations.

Goal
Create a minimal-but-sufficient set of metrics and burn-rate alerts that:
- power the failover signal dashboards and game day success criteria
- reduce time-to-...

title: Incident-bot: notify #incidents when overload protection activates (shed/admission control)
preview:
Add structured incident-bot notifications when hot-route capacity protection activates so on-call can quickly distinguish expected best-effort shedding from enterprise-impacting overload events.

We need notifications for:
- sustained admission control (best-effort gated) on protected routes
- shed-rate threshold ex...
text:
Add structured incident-bot notifications when hot-route capacity protection activates so on-call can quickly distinguish expected best-effort shedding from enterprise-impacting overload events.

We need notifications for:
- sustained admission control (best-effort gated) on protected routes
- shed-rate threshold exceeded (active dropping) on protected routes

Primary destination: #incidents (and optionally #eng-oncall as a lower-noise mirror for early canary). Notifications must be deduped, rate-limited, and include links to the relevant dashboard + runbook section.

title: Status page metrics backfill job (historical component health for incident summaries + trends)
preview:
## Context
We’re improving status page transparency by moving to a more granular component taxonomy (product surface x region x capability) and publishing higher-quality post-incident summaries quickly.

A recurring gap: we can draft incident summaries from the incident timeline, but we don’t have reliable historica...
text:
## Context
We’re improving status page transparency by moving to a more granular component taxonomy (product surface x region x capability) and publishing higher-quality post-incident summaries quickly.

A recurring gap: we can draft incident summaries from the incident timeline, but we don’t have reliable historical component health rollups (error rate + latency) for the impacted components over the incident window and for pre/post comparisons. This is slowing Comms Leads down and causing “hand-wavy” impact statements.

This ticket adds a backfill job that populates historical component health metrics so:
- post-incident summaries can include concrete impact metrics (e.g., peak error rate, p95 latency, duration)
- we can trend component health over time and audit status accuracy

## Goals
1) Backfill component-level health rollups for a defined history window (initial proposal: 90 da...

title: Lightweight approval workflow for customer-facing status updates during Sev0/Sev1 incidents
preview:
Goal
Implement a lightweight approval workflow for customer-facing Statuspage updates when incident severity is high (Sev0/Sev1). This is part of the Status page transparency upgrades initiative and is intended to reduce "unreviewed" public messaging during fast-moving events while keeping time-to-first-update (TTFU...
text:
Goal
Implement a lightweight approval workflow for customer-facing Statuspage updates when incident severity is high (Sev0/Sev1). This is part of the Status page transparency upgrades initiative and is intended to reduce "unreviewed" public messaging during fast-moving events while keeping time-to-first-update (TTFU) low.

Problem
We’ve repeatedly seen two failure modes during high-severity incidents:
1) Updates go out quickly but contain incorrect component/region scope, overly certain root-cause language, or customer-sensitive detail.
2) Teams delay posting because they’re unsure who should sign off, which increases tickets and erodes trust.

Non-goals
- Build a heavyweight change-approval system or require approvals for Sev2+.
- Require approvals for internal-only updates (e.g., #incidents threads).
- Replace Comms Lead ownership; the workflow should support Comms Lead, not create ...

title: Auto-generate publishable post-incident summaries from incident timeline + metrics (with review workflow)
preview:
Problem
Customers (and Support/CS) are asking for consistent, fast, and actionable post-incident summaries. Today, writing publishable summaries is manual and slow, often blocked on reconstructing timelines and pulling metrics. This results in (a) delays and (b) inconsistent content/tone, and (c) occasional over-dis...
text:
Problem
Customers (and Support/CS) are asking for consistent, fast, and actionable post-incident summaries. Today, writing publishable summaries is manual and slow, often blocked on reconstructing timelines and pulling metrics. This results in (a) delays and (b) inconsistent content/tone, and (c) occasional over-disclosure risk.

Goal
Generate a draft, publishable post-incident summary automatically from:
1) the incident timeline (state changes, mitigations, comms events), and
2) a small set of component/region health metrics (errors/latency + duration),
then route it through a lightweight review workflow before it is posted publicly.

Non-goals
- Not generating a full internal postmortem (deep causal chain, contributing factors, action items). This is for customer-facing summaries only.
- Not auto-publishing without a human review step.
- Not making root cause claims beyond what the ...

title: Implement status page component granularity (new taxonomy + shared registry)
preview:
Goal
Implement the new public status page component taxonomy (product surface x region x capability where applicable) as a shared, versioned registry/config and ensure consistent usage across:
- incident intake + comms (incident-bot)
- observability labels/dashboards + SLO tooling
- status page publishing (Statuspag...
text:
Goal
Implement the new public status page component taxonomy (product surface x region x capability where applicable) as a shared, versioned registry/config and ensure consistent usage across:
- incident intake + comms (incident-bot)
- observability labels/dashboards + SLO tooling
- status page publishing (Statuspage component IDs and grouping)

Why
Recent incidents (e.g., US-East streaming degradation) exposed that our current status components are too coarse ("API") which led to inaccurate/misleading public updates and increased Support load. Customers have explicitly asked for per-surface components (Streaming vs Chat vs Embeddings) and per-region visibility.

Scope
1) Component registry / source-of-truth
- Create a single registry file that defines:
 - stable component_id (machine ID)
 - display_name (public label)
 - product_area (hosted-api | console | dedicated | private)
 - ca...

title: Map internal services/metrics to public status page components (registry + routing rules + tests)
preview:
We need a deterministic mapping layer from internal service identifiers + metric tags to the canonical public status components (product surface x region x capability), so incident-bot + dashboards + audit tooling can consistently mark the correct components during incidents.

This ticket implements:
- A component r...
text:
We need a deterministic mapping layer from internal service identifiers + metric tags to the canonical public status components (product surface x region x capability), so incident-bot + dashboards + audit tooling can consistently mark the correct components during incidents.

This ticket implements:
- A component registry lookup (component_id, display name, ownership) used as the output contract
- A mapping function from internal signals (gateway/service names, request routes, model/task type, region) -> component_id(s)
- Tests + fixtures to prevent regressions and catch "embeddings misclassified as chat" style bugs before they hit the status page

Background:
Recent incidents showed we can’t rely on manual component selection when services are shared and failure modes are capability-specific (e.g., streaming disconnects only). We also need consistent behavior across:
- gateway metri...

title: Add component-level SLO burn-rate alerts aligned to public status page components
preview:
## Context
As part of the Status page transparency upgrades initiative, we’re tightening the loop between internal health signals and customer-facing status updates.

Today, burn alerts are generally service-centric (gateway, scheduler, etc.), which slows down:
- time-to-detection (we often detect “gateway SLO” but ...
text:
## Context
As part of the Status page transparency upgrades initiative, we’re tightening the loop between internal health signals and customer-facing status updates.

Today, burn alerts are generally service-centric (gateway, scheduler, etc.), which slows down:
- time-to-detection (we often detect “gateway SLO” but need manual translation to “Streaming (US-East) is degraded”)
- time-to-first-update (TTFU) because Comms Lead/IC need extra verification and component selection

This ticket adds component-level SLO burn-rate alerts keyed to the *public status page component taxonomy* (component_id + region + capability where applicable) so on-call can quickly understand customer impact and choose the right status components.

## Goal
Create and route burn-rate alerts that match public status components (Hosted API, Streaming, Embeddings, Reranking, Console where applicable), split by regi...

title: SRE dashboards + alerting for overload protection (load shedding + breakers)
preview:
Goal
Ship first-class SRE dashboards + alert rules for the Hosted overload protection system (circuit breakers + load shedding), and wire them into oncall paging with a structured false-positive review loop.

Context
As we roll out admission control (gateway) + routing fallback + runtime backpressure, we need consis...
text:
Goal
Ship first-class SRE dashboards + alert rules for the Hosted overload protection system (circuit breakers + load shedding), and wire them into oncall paging with a structured false-positive review loop.

Context
As we roll out admission control (gateway) + routing fallback + runtime backpressure, we need consistent visibility and actionable paging to prevent “silent overload” (tail latency + timeouts) and to avoid alert noise. Dashboards should support both: (1) real-time incident triage and (2) post-incident/threshold tuning.

Scope (this ticket)
1) Dashboards (Grafana) in observability-pack
- Overload overview dashboard (by region):
 - Shed rate (% and RPS) broken down by reason bucket (gateway admission vs runtime saturation vs dependency health) with low-cardinality labels.
 - Breaker state transitions (closed/open/half-open) rate and current open count.
 - Correlated request...
tags: Hosted circuit breakers + load shedding (Reliability Program)

title: Run KMS outage game day (timeout/throttling/AccessDenied) + capture follow-ups
preview:
## Goal
Validate end-to-end behavior and operator readiness when KMS is degraded or misconfigured across Hosted/Dedicated/Private deployment modes.

We want to confirm:
- KMS wrapper timeouts/retry budgets/circuit breaker prevent retry storms + cascading failures
- Error taxonomy is consistent (logs/metrics), and on...
text:
## Goal
Validate end-to-end behavior and operator readiness when KMS is degraded or misconfigured across Hosted/Dedicated/Private deployment modes.

We want to confirm:
- KMS wrapper timeouts/retry budgets/circuit breaker prevent retry storms + cascading failures
- Error taxonomy is consistent (logs/metrics), and on-call can quickly triage
- Dashboards/alerts are actionable (not noisy) and point to correct remediation
- Runbooks cover the most common failure modes with concrete steps
- Private installer preflight checks catch the “obvious” misconfigs before customers hit runtime failures

## Scope / systems under test
- Control plane: key version config/dual-read cutover flags
- Data plane: envelope decrypt path (runtime)
- Observability: KMS dependency health dashboard + alerts
- Private installer: KMS preflight validation (where available)

References:
- Confluence game day doc: sou...
tags: Security & Compliance Foundation: KMS integration hardening + rotation docs

title: Incident-bot: Statuspage integration (draft + publish updates, capture impacted components)
preview:
Background
We’re upgrading public status page transparency (component granularity + consistent comms + publishable post-incident summaries). Today incident-bot helps with incident intake and internal coordination, but status updates are still largely manual and inconsistent (TTFU misses, incorrect component selectio...
text:
Background
We’re upgrading public status page transparency (component granularity + consistent comms + publishable post-incident summaries). Today incident-bot helps with incident intake and internal coordination, but status updates are still largely manual and inconsistent (TTFU misses, incorrect component selection, copy drift).

This ticket adds a Statuspage integration into incident-bot so we can:
1) capture component/region/capability impact fields as part of incident intake, and keep them current
2) generate status update drafts (Investigating/Identified/Mitigating/Monitoring/Resolved) from incident state + known facts
3) publish updates to Statuspage via API with audit logging + safe retry semantics
4) support an approval step for Sev0/Sev1 where required (Support lead or IC), without adding significant on-call burden

Goals
- Reduce time-to-first-update (TTFU) by making it eas...

title: Runtime: export queue depth + scheduler lag metrics for overload protection (low-cardinality)
preview:
We need a small set of runtime-exported capacity signals that can be used by routing/admission control to trigger tier-aware overload protection (gate best-effort first, preserve enterprise budgets). The signals must be stable, cheap to compute, and safe from high-cardinality blowups.

This ticket implements runtime...
text:
We need a small set of runtime-exported capacity signals that can be used by routing/admission control to trigger tier-aware overload protection (gate best-effort first, preserve enterprise budgets). The signals must be stable, cheap to compute, and safe from high-cardinality blowups.

This ticket implements runtime instrumentation + export for:
- Queue depth (per model pool and optionally per route group)
- Scheduler lag (time-in-queue / dispatch delay)
- Optional supporting signals to validate correctness (active requests, batch size, kv cache pressure) while keeping tag set bounded

Constraints:
- Must not add meaningful overhead to the hot path (avoid per-request tagging work)
- Cardinality must be explicitly bounded (no tenant_id/customer_id, no raw route, no prompt length buckets at high resolution)
- Must support per-route aggregation via a small route_group / endpoint_group di...

title: Game day plan: predictive autoscaling failure injection + success criteria
preview:
## Summary
Run a structured game day for Dedicated predictive autoscaling (with customer min/max bounds) prior to GA expansion. We will inject common failures (forecast outages/staleness, traffic ramps/spikes, stuck scale-down, capacity shortages) and verify:
- safety invariants (never exceed max; never drop below m...
text:
## Summary
Run a structured game day for Dedicated predictive autoscaling (with customer min/max bounds) prior to GA expansion. We will inject common failures (forecast outages/staleness, traffic ramps/spikes, stuck scale-down, capacity shortages) and verify:
- safety invariants (never exceed max; never drop below min; no scale-to-zero regressions)
- deterministic, auditable decisioning (requested desired, bounded desired, reasons)
- alerting + dashboards provide actionable signals
- on-call mitigations are safe and documented

## Background / context
Predictive mode biases the autoscaler using forecasted demand; reactive signals must still override incorrect predictions (while respecting bounds). During pilot we saw at least one unsafe edge case when predictor returned null (hotfix landed), and we need confidence we can safely operate under partial outages and noisy inputs.

Relevant...

title: Document Private observability day-2 runbooks + Support playbooks (minimum pack)
preview:

Private customers (VPC + on-prem) need a *supported minimum* day-2 playbook for the new Private Observability Minimum Pack so Customer Support + SRE can debug issues without bespoke tribal knowledge.

This ticket covers:
- Confluence runbooks (day-2 ops + troubleshooting)
- Support playbooks (what to ask ...
text:
## Context
Private customers (VPC + on-prem) need a *supported minimum* day-2 playbook for the new Private Observability Minimum Pack so Customer Support + SRE can debug issues without bespoke tribal knowledge.

This ticket covers:
- Confluence runbooks (day-2 ops + troubleshooting)
- Support playbooks (what to ask for, first-response checklist, escalation criteria)
- Linking runbooks from alert annotations + known-issues page

## Goals
1) Standardize post-install verification and post-upgrade verification steps.
2) Document common failure modes seen in early deployments (dashboards empty, scrape failures, noisy alerts, log forwarder backpressure, exporter auth issues).
3) Establish a consistent escalation path and required artifacts for handoff (Support -> SRE/Eng).
4) Ensure guidance is safe for Private: no instructions that encourage logging request bodies/PII; call out redaction/r...

title: Rollback state machine + idempotency rules for Private upgrades (handle partial steps safely)
preview:
Context
Private upgrades are moving to a staged, auditable workflow with prechecks + backups + deterministic rollback. This ticket defines the rollback state machine and idempotency rules across installer + Helm phases, including how we represent partially-applied steps and how rollback should behave when we’re mid-...
text:
Context
Private upgrades are moving to a staged, auditable workflow with prechecks + backups + deterministic rollback. This ticket defines the rollback state machine and idempotency rules across installer + Helm phases, including how we represent partially-applied steps and how rollback should behave when we’re mid-upgrade.

Problem statement
In-place upgrades frequently fail in the middle (DB migration lock timeout, chart rollout stuck, artifact mismatch, node disruption). Our old guidance was either “try again” or “restore from backup manually,” which caused inconsistent outcomes and longer MTTR. We need:
- a single source of truth for upgrade/rollback progress
- deterministic behavior under retries
- explicit handling of partial upgrade states (some steps applied, others not)
- clear operator UX + logs suitable for support bundles

Goals
1) Define a rollback state machine that can ...

title: Support playbook + standardized denial reason codes for Dedicated Burst admission
preview:
Problem
Support/CS are seeing multiple flavors of "burst denied" behavior during beta (429s, overload, routing fallback, and occasionally silent slowdown). Without standardized denial reason codes and a consistent customer-facing explanation, Support cannot reliably answer:
- Why was my burst denied?
- Is this expec...
text:
Problem
Support/CS are seeing multiple flavors of "burst denied" behavior during beta (429s, overload, routing fallback, and occasionally silent slowdown). Without standardized denial reason codes and a consistent customer-facing explanation, Support cannot reliably answer:
- Why was my burst denied?
- Is this expected (limits/guardrails) vs. an incident?
- What should we do next (retry/backoff vs. request config change vs. escalate to SRE/Fleet)?

This ticket standardizes the denial reason codes emitted by the Burst Admission Controller and ensures:
1) The reason codes are surfaced consistently through the gateway/routing path.
2) Support tooling exists to explain “why” for a specific request/tenant/time window.
3) A Support playbook exists with customer-ready language, troubleshooting steps, and escalation paths.

Scope
A. Denial reason code taxonomy (v1)
- Define canonical machine ...
